items.title: DV shelter death inquest hears from top police, child safety representatives

children.content: A coronial inquest hears that Northern Territory authorities assessed that a father posed "no risk" to his child, despite allegations he repeatedly abused his partner and shook the baby.

image.altText: Darwin local court interior.

items.title: Boy, 16, killed, two others injured in Wadeye quad bike crash

children.content: A 16-year-old boy has been killed in a quad bike crash that also injured a 12-year-old boy and a 19-year-old man in Wadeye on Wednesday.

image.altText: Close up image of an NT Police sign, behind barbed wire fence, with left of frame slightly obstructed by palm tree leaves.

items.title: Kingpin's private phone calls break open Mongols' 'culture of silence'

children.content: Detectives say bikies are recruiting inside Northern Territory prisons, as a kingpin's tapped private phone calls offer a rare glimpse into the underground world of outlaw motorcycle gangs.

gallery.altText: A red white and blue skull embroidered into patches sewn into vests.

thumbnail.altText: A red white and blue skull embroidered into patches sewn into vests.

items.title: 'How many beers have you had?' Flood evacuees claim racial profiling

children.content: Flood evacuees from remote Northern Territory communities say they are being "treated like prisoners" by security guards working at their emergency accommodation centre.

image.altText: Signs attached to black fencing reads: no alcohol, no visitors

items.title: Police 'not fully prepared' for violent scene they were called to, NT inquest hears

children.content: An inquest hears evidence from a senior police officer who ran into a room to disarm a mother who was self-harming inside a Darwin crisis accommodation facility, describing the incident as "one of the worst days" of her policing career.

image.altText: A police car outside the Darwin Local Court.

items.title: 101yo Bombing of Darwin veteran wants Australians to know what unfolded

children.content: At 101 years old, Bombing of Darwin survivor Terrance Byrne wants Australians to know exactly what unfolded during the World War II attack on Darwin — still the single largest foreign attack on Australian soil.

gallery.altText: An older man sitting in front of table of framed black and white pictures.
A black and white photo shows a huge crater in the ground, alongside three undamaged oil tanks located near the coastline.
An army crew operating a large searchlight, the uniformed men are looking towards and pointing up at the night sky.

items.title: New $30.5m funding package to help repair NT roads damaged by flooding

children.content: The Northern Territory government has announced $30.5 million in funding to repair roads damaged by wet season flooding across the territory.

image.altText: A remote road collapsed to one side.

items.title: Housing plan aims to attract more students to international 'gateway' city

children.content: A towering 14-storey addition to Darwin's skyline is expected to ease housing stress for university students, with $70 million invested to tackle the city's competitive rental market.

image.altText: A sketch of a multi-storey building based in the city, the CDU logo can be seen on the side of the accommodation tower.

items.title: Central Australian community sues government over climate-unsafe housing

children.content: Residents of the Central Australian community of Papunya say scorching outback summers are killing their people in unsafe housing. Now they're suing the Northern Territory government to try to force change.

gallery.altText: A drone shot of a small remote community, which features a small number of homes built on the red dirt landscape.
An Indigenous woman and two children sitting on a mattress that rests on the flood inside a home.
A box air conditioner on the outside of a house, balanced on an ironing board and a table.

items.title: Australia must 'get serious' about defence to be better ally to US, Hastie says

children.content: Liberal frontbencher Andrew Hastie uses a speech ahead of Anzac Day to warn Australia must bring more to its alliance with the US, and appreciate that it is a relationship built on strategic value, not sentimentality. Follow live.

image.altText: Andrew Hastie press gallery doorstop

children.content: The warning notice to the so-called "finfluencers" relates to suspected provision of unlicensed financial advice, including promoting claims of guaranteed returns.

articles.title: Viva Energy boss says more refineries needed for secure fuel supply

children.content: The Viva Energy refinery provides about 10 per cent of Australia's fuel. Its chief, Scott Wyatt, chats to Alan Kohler about the company's future in the wake of the Geelong fire.

items.title: 'This is now terminal': UK braces for prospect of sixth PM in seven years

children.content: Sir Keir Starmer, who led Labour to a landslide general election victory in July 2024, has become so unpopular that even some colleagues are framing his demise as a case of not if, but when.

items.title: Ancient fossils reveal when the iconic Twelve Apostles formed

children.content: An analysis of tiny fossils of sea creatures found in rock samples around the Port Campbell region in south-western Victoria has refined our understanding of how the Twelve Apostles formed.

items.title: 'Sad for us': Hannah Clarke's parents step back from advocacy

children.content: After their daughter and three grandchildren were murdered, Sue and Lloyd Clarke began a charity to improve awareness of coercive control. Now they've decided they need to step back for their own health.

image.altText: Hannah Clarke, hugs her son Trey Baxter, while standing on a beach.

items.title: Which states get a public holiday for Anzac Day?

children.content: Anzac Day is observed on April 25 every year. This year that date falls on a Saturday, but that doesn't mean a long weekend for everyone.

image.altText: poppies on a cenotaph

items.title: Jane Fonda was in her 40s when she changed the way we exercise

children.content: In the 1980s, the fitness industry as we know it was just getting started. One of the most important people in developing the way we exercise today was Jane Fonda, who released a best-selling aerobics video in 1982.

gallery.altText: Actress Jane Fonda in a yellow exercise outfit stretching with her left foot pointed upward in the air.

items.title: Figure skating teen makes history for Australia

children.content: At just 15, Hana Bath became the first Australian female to ever land a triple axel in competition. The rising star now has her eye on the 2030 Winter Olympics.

items.title: Weekly news quiz: Trump's eleventh-hour announcement, a disabled bird and a speedy humanoid robot

items.title: Now James Valentine is gone, I'll let you in on the secret of his success

children.content: Children grew up with him, adults grew old with him, he kept us connected to our city, the world and each other. James helped us make sense of life and gave us permission to laugh at the absurdity of it all — and ourselves.

articles.title: James Valentine swapped microphones — and found a new audience who loved him

children.content: James Valentine started his career on the stage, but it was his time behind the microphone in the studio and an interactive style that came to define him.

image.altText: a man smiles while holding his chin

articles.title: James Valentine, beloved ABC talkback radio star, dies aged 64

children.content: The presenter and professional saxophone player is being remembered for his ability to explore the humour and oddities of daily life on the airwaves.

image.altText: a man sitting down smiling while clasping his hands together

gallery.altText: James Valentine, wearing a read jumper and black headphones, smiling and talking into a microphone.

items.title: 'They deserve death': Officer used police system to monitor wife

children.content: Text messages read in court set out how a Tasmania Police officer monitored his wife's movements, including via an internal dispatching system, as part of a 15-month family violence matter.

image.altText: A text message that says: "Those people on your shift are fucking dead."

items.title: The legacy of once-banned soldiers that hides in plain sight

children.content: Thousands of Indian soldiers served alongside Anzac forces at Gallipoli, leaving their legacy in the "puggaree" — a traditional Indian headwrap that was adapted to adorn the slouch hats worn by the Australian army.

image.altText: Six men with red and white striped turbans in khaki uniforms with red and white around their waist and long white socks.

items.title: Why China is recruiting fishermen to 'maintain presence' — not to fish

children.content: Off China's southern coast, hundreds of fishing boats sit idle. The crews aren't there to fish, but rather to "maintain presence" as part of a shadowy, state-funded fleet.

image.altText: A satellite image giving a birdseye view of boats scattered across a bay.

items.title: Sheraz risked his life translating in Afghanistan. His family paid the price

children.content: Sheraz worked with Australian and US troops in Afghanistan, as a result his family were targeted by the Taliban. This Anzac Day he's being heroed.

items.title: Adelaide Festival appoints new Writers' Week director in wake of crisis

items.title: War on hoons

children.content: You might have heard of hooning – in which people — usually young men — drift, race, and do burnouts on public roads.
It happens across the country and has been around for decades. But, it’s getting bigger, louder and more visible.
So are police crackdowns. Fines, car seizures and jail terms are all on the cards for people caught hooning.
Despite the laws and serious dangers, people still do it. But, why?

image.altText: Blue backgroun with white text. Text reads Inside Australia's illegal drift racing world. ABC News Loop logo
